---
title: "1.1 The Importance of Backtesting"
subtitle: "PDF notes | Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "NotoSerifCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    mainfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSerifCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    sansfont: "NotoSansCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    sansfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSansCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    monofont: "MapleMono-NF-CN-Regular.ttf"
    monofontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Bold.ttf"
      - "ItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Italic.ttf"
      - "BoldItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-BoldItalic.ttf"
    mathfont: "latinmodern-math.otf"
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    filecolor: blue
include-in-header:
  text: |
    \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{title}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{author}{\rmfamily}
    \addtokomafont{date}{\rmfamily}
---

# Backtesting Is a Rehearsal, Not a Scoreboard

Backtesting feeds historical data into a trading strategy to ask how the
strategy would have performed. That definition sounds simple, but the useful
part is conditional: the historical test matters only if it rehearses the rule
that could later run live.

This is why a backtest is more than an equity curve. The equity curve shows the
result of a simulated trading process. The backtest itself has to define the
process: what data were known, which price field was used, which order would
have been sent, where it would have been routed, and whether the trade could
have existed at the intended size. If those details are missing, the chart may
describe a strategy that cannot be executed.

# Published Results Still Need Independent Backtesting

Independent backtesting matters even when the original strategy comes from a
trusted publication. A published article usually has to protect the main idea
from too much operational detail. That is reasonable for communication, but it
is dangerous for implementation.

For example, "buy at the open" could mean a market-on-open auction order, or it
could mean a market order sent shortly after the open. A strategy involving the
E-mini S&P 500 future may depend on whether the order is timed around the 4:00
p.m. stock-market close or the 4:15 p.m. futures close. A rule that "triggers
on price" still has to specify whether that price is the bid, the ask, the last
trade, the high, or the low.

These choices are not cosmetic. They can change both the backtest and the live
profitability of the strategy. Rebuilding the strategy ourselves forces those
choices into the open and turns an informal rule into an executable
specification.

# The Backtest Should Stay Close to the Execution System

Backtesting and automated execution are linked because both need the same
implementation assumptions. Ideally, the backtesting program can be transformed
into a live execution program with the same strategy logic. In that design, the
historical feed is swapped for a live market feed, but the decision rule stays
fixed.

This discipline reduces a common failure mode: the research code tests one
strategy while the production code trades another. Shared logic does not remove
every source of error, but it makes timing, order type, venue, and price-field
assumptions easier to inspect. It also makes accidental look-ahead logic harder
to hide because the live system cannot request tomorrow's data.

# Use the Backtest to Search for Impossible Trades

Once the implementation details are explicit, the next job is to look for
pitfalls. A long-short stock strategy has to ask whether the short positions
were borrowable at a reasonable size. An intermarket futures pair has to ask
whether the two market closes occurred at the same time. A strategy that uses
open or close prices has to ask whether those prices match the order type it
would actually send.

Most backtesting pitfalls are dangerous because they bias the result upward.
They make the strategy look more profitable than it could have been, not merely
noisier. Pitfall analysis therefore belongs before we trust the performance
statistics, not after we are already impressed by them.

# True Out-of-Sample Testing Begins After the Rule Freezes

Backtesting a published strategy can create a cleaner out-of-sample test than
the one claimed inside the original article. Before publication, the author may
have tested a validation period, disliked the result, changed a parameter, and
tested again. After enough revisions, the validation data have effectively
become part of the research process.

After publication, the rule is much harder to revise quietly. New data that
arrive after that point can test the published rule as it stands. Weak
post-publication performance does not prove misconduct or carelessness, but it
does raise an important possibility: the original result may have depended on a
limited historical sample.

# Experiment Freely, Then Validate Strictly

Backtesting is also a way to improve a strategy. The research cycle begins with
a hypothesis about a market inefficiency, converts that hypothesis into code,
tests it on historical data, and revises it when the evidence is not good
enough. Small changes can matter: a different lookback window, an entry at the
open instead of the close, or a different price field can materially change the
result.

That experimentation is legitimate during research. The boundary matters. Once
the rule is frozen, the next test should evaluate the strategy rather than give
the researcher another chance to tune it. A credible research process therefore
allows iteration before the validation boundary and protects the validation
period after the boundary.

# Review Checklist

- What hypothesis about market behavior is the backtest testing?
- Which instrument, venue, timestamp, order type, and price field does the rule
  use?
- Could the same strategy logic run on live market data?
- Are borrow limits, closing-time mismatches, venue constraints, and other
  strategy-specific feasibility checks represented?
- Is the post-publication, walk-forward, or final validation period protected
  from further tuning?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [Chapter overview](../../../index.qmd)
- Next: [1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting](../02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/pdf-notes.qmd)
